heroOverview: Cowpox is a localised viral skin infection caused by Cowpox virus (CPXV), an Orthopoxvirus whose natural reservoir is rodents — particularly bank voles and wood mice — not cattle, despite the name. Humans acquire cowpox almost exclusively through direct contact with infected animals; domestic cats are now the most common source of infection in European countries, serving as accidental dead-end hosts that carry the virus from wild rodents to people and their pets. Cowpox is rare — the United Kingdom and Germany together report only about 40–80 human cases per year in the published literature — and most infections in immunocompetent adults are self-limiting, resolving within 3–10 weeks without specific treatment. The infection matters medically for two reasons: in immunocompromised patients it can cause progressive, disseminated, and sometimes fatal disease; and historically, a cowpox-like orthopoxvirus vaccination was the basis of Edward Jenner's 1796 discovery that inoculation with cowpox material protected against smallpox — the foundational event of modern vaccinology.

keyStats.prevalence: Rare; approximately 40–80 human cases reported annually across Europe (UK and Germany contribute most published data); true incidence likely higher due to underreporting
keyStats.demographics: All ages susceptible; domestic cat owners and handlers disproportionately affected; children bitten by cats represent a significant group; exotic animal (zoo) workers also at risk
keyStats.avgAge: No strong age peak; cases reported from childhood through older adulthood; more severe disease in atopic or immunocompromised individuals at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Endemic in Eurasia (Western and Central Europe); not established in North America or Australasia

definition: Cowpox (ICD-10: B08.010) is a skin infection caused by Cowpox virus (CPXV), a large double-stranded DNA virus in the Orthopoxvirus genus, which also includes Variola (smallpox), Monkeypox virus, and vaccinia. Despite its name, cows are not the natural reservoir and bovine infections are rare; the primary hosts are wild rodents, particularly bank voles (Myodes glareolus) and wood mice (Apodemus sylvaticus) in Europe and parts of Asia. Domestic cats, rats, dogs, and zoo animals (elephants, rhinoceroses, big cats) become infected when in contact with rodents and can transmit the virus to humans. Human infection typically produces one or a few painful, ulcerating skin lesions that pass through a characteristic sequence of stages — a macule or papule progressing to a vesicle, pustule, haemorrhagic crust (the 'black pox' eschar that is highly distinctive), and finally healing scar — at the site of animal contact, most often on the hands, face, or arm. Regional lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms (fever, malaise) accompany the cutaneous lesion and resolve spontaneously over 3–10 weeks in most cases. In immunocompromised patients — including those with HIV, atopic eczema, organ transplant recipients, and those on immunosuppressive therapy — cowpox can disseminate widely, causing multiple lesions, pneumonia, and a fatality rate that, while not precisely quantified in large series, has been reported in several published European cases. Cross-reactive immunity from prior smallpox vaccination (before ~1980) or mpox vaccination (MVA-BN) confers substantial protection against cowpox.

typesClassification.description: The most common form in immunocompetent individuals; one or a few lesions at the inoculation site, usually a bite or scratch, progressing through macule, papule, vesicle, pustule, and haemorrhagic black eschar before healing over 3–10 weeks.

Occurs almost exclusively in immunocompromised patients; multiple lesions at distant sites, potential visceral involvement including pneumonia, high morbidity, and documented fatalities. Lesions may not follow the typical self-limiting course.

Analogous to eczema vaccinatum from the vaccinia vaccine; pre-existing atopic dermatitis disrupts the skin barrier and can allow widespread cutaneous cowpox infection mimicking Kaposi varicelliform eruption; may be severe and require antiviral treatment.

primarySymptoms: A single painful, erythematous papule or macule at the site of animal contact (typically bite, scratch, or lick site on the hands, face, or forearm) appearing 9–10 days after exposure.
Rapid progression of the initial papule through a vesicular (fluid-filled blister) stage over 3–5 days, filling with increasing amounts of clear then cloudy fluid.

Formation of a deep pustule that becomes haemorrhagic (blood-filled), creating a striking dark red to black crust — the characteristic 'black eschar' or 'nodular lesion' considered pathognomonic of cowpox.
The haemorrhagic eschar develops an indurated (hardened) inflamed base up to 3–4 cm in diameter with surrounding erythema and oedema, resembling an anthrax or orf lesion.
Painful regional lymphadenopathy (swollen, tender lymph nodes) draining the area of the lesion — typically axillary nodes for hand/arm lesions and cervical nodes for face/neck lesions.
Systemic symptoms accompanying the pustular stage: fever (typically 38–39°C), malaise, fatigue, and headache lasting 1–2 weeks.
Local pain and tenderness at the lesion site, often described as disproportionately severe relative to lesion size, particularly in the pustular and haemorrhagic stage.
Gradual crusting and drying of the lesion over weeks 4–6, with a deep, firm, attached black eschar that separates spontaneously.
Residual scar following eschar separation, typically a round, pitted or atrophic scar analogous to a smallpox vaccination scar.
In severe or disseminated disease (immunocompromised): multiple lesions at different body sites, mucous membrane involvement, conjunctival lesions, or pulmonary symptoms.
earlyWarningSigns: A red, sore papule at the site of a recent cat bite, cat scratch, or contact with a rat or exotic animal.
Progressive enlargement and darkening of an initial skin lesion 7–14 days after animal contact.
Swollen, tender lymph nodes in the armpit or neck developing alongside a skin lesion on the hand or face.
emergencySigns: Multiple skin lesions appearing away from the original bite site in anyone with a weakened immune system.
Shortness of breath, cough, or chest pain alongside a cowpox skin lesion — suggesting pulmonary spread.
Rapid worsening of widespread eczematous skin with new vesicles in a patient with atopic dermatitis (eczema cowpox).
Eye involvement — vesicles or ulcers on the eyelid or conjunctiva with eye pain or visual change — require same-day ophthalmological assessment.

causes.description: Domestic cats hunting wild rodents act as the bridge host that carries CPXV from its natural rodent reservoir to humans; cats develop lesions on their paws and muzzle and transmit the virus through bites, scratches, or licking open skin. Cats are now responsible for the majority of human cowpox cases in Western Europe.

causes.cause: Contact with wild or captive rodents
causes.description: Bank voles, wood mice, and other wild small rodents are the natural reservoir hosts for CPXV in Europe; they maintain the virus without showing significant disease. Humans and cats in rural or peri-urban environments with high rodent populations are at greatest risk of spillover from this reservoir.

causes.cause: Contact with infected zoo or exotic animals
causes.description: Elephants, cheetahs, lions, rhinoceroses, circus animals, and other captive exotic species can be infected by CPXV from rodents and transmit the virus to animal handlers, zoo workers, or veterinarians. Several European zoo-related outbreaks affecting keeper staff have been reported.

Historical transmission from cattle udders to milkmaids' hands gave the disease its name; modern bovine cowpox is very rare because CPXV circulates primarily in rodents, not in cattle. Occasional occupational cases in dairy workers or veterinarians still occur.

CPXV can survive in dried scabs and on environmental surfaces; indirect transmission through contact with virus-contaminated bedding, clothing, or animal care equipment has been documented, though it is a less common route than direct animal contact.

riskFactors.description: Domestic cats are the single most commonly identified source of human cowpox in Europe; cat owners who have cats with access to the outdoors and who handle cats with suspicious skin lesions are at highest risk.

riskFactors.factor: No prior smallpox vaccination or orthopoxvirus immunity
riskFactors.description: People vaccinated against smallpox (before ~1980) retain partial cross-protective immunity against cowpox; individuals born after the cessation of routine smallpox vaccination have no baseline orthopoxvirus immunity and are fully susceptible.

Disrupted skin barrier in atopic dermatitis allows wider inoculation of CPXV and the potential development of disseminated cutaneous cowpox analogous to eczema vaccinatum; the use of immunosuppressive topical therapies (corticosteroids, tacrolimus) in eczema further increases severity risk.

Loss of cell-mediated immunity removes the primary defence against poxvirus spread; case reports of fatal disseminated cowpox and cowpox pneumonia have been documented almost exclusively in immunocompromised patients in the European literature.

riskFactors.factor: Zoo work or exotic animal handling
riskFactors.description: Animal handlers, zookeepers, and veterinarians working with elephants, big cats, and other exotic animals are at occupational risk; pre-exposure vaccination with MVA-BN is recommended for high-risk occupational groups.

riskFactors.factor: Rural or peri-urban environment with high rodent density
riskFactors.description: Higher local rodent prevalence increases the chance that cats and dogs in the area become infected and subsequently expose their owners; autumn and winter are associated with increased rodent activity and higher CPXV transmission to cats.

affectedDemographics: Domestic cat owners and their household contacts — the primary risk group in Western Europe
Rural residents and those in areas with high wild rodent populations
Zoo workers, veterinarians, and laboratory animal handlers
Children who play with cats or handle pet rodents
Immunocompromised patients at risk of severe or disseminated disease
People with atopic eczema at risk of eczema cowpox
diagnosisOverview: Cowpox is diagnosed by combining a compatible clinical picture — a painful haemorrhagic pustular or eschar lesion at a site of animal contact, with regional lymphadenopathy and systemic symptoms — with laboratory confirmation. The distinctive dark haemorrhagic crust on an indurated base is strongly suggestive in a patient with a relevant animal exposure. The main differentials are orf (parapoxvirus from sheep or goats, causing a similar but typically more warty eschar in farmers), anthrax (painless black eschar without vesicular progression, in people with animal hide or wool exposure), and milker's nodule (also a parapoxvirus causing smooth, firm nodules in dairy workers). Herpes simplex with extensive necrosis and cutaneous leishmaniasis should also be considered depending on geography. Laboratory confirmation relies on PCR testing of swab material from the lesion (vesicle fluid or the inner surface of the crust); CPXV-specific PCR distinguishes cowpox from other orthopoxviruses. Electron microscopy of vesicle fluid shows characteristic brick-shaped orthopoxvirus particles. Viral culture at reference laboratories and whole-genome sequencing can characterise the strain. Serology (orthopoxvirus-specific IgM and IgG) is useful as a supportive tool but cross-reacts across the genus. Full blood count and immunological screening (HIV test, immunoglobulin levels, lymphocyte subsets) should be performed to identify previously unrecognised immunodeficiency in patients with unusually severe or slow-healing cowpox.

diagnosticTests.test: PCR from lesion swab or vesicle fluid
diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold-standard test; detects Cowpox virus DNA and distinguishes CPXV from other orthopoxviruses and parapoxviruses; suitable for active vesicles, pustule fluid, or material from beneath the eschar crust.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician swabs the lesion base or aspirates vesicle fluid; the sample is sent to a reference virology laboratory for PCR; results typically return in 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid confirmation of Orthopoxvirus genus by detecting characteristic brick-shaped viral particles; does not distinguish cowpox from other orthopoxviruses but confirms poxvirus aetiology within hours.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Vesicle fluid is aspirated with a fine needle, fixed immediately, and transported to a facility with electron microscopy capability; results available within hours at reference centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detection of acute IgM (appears from day 5 of rash) and later IgG antibody response; useful when swab material is unavailable or to confirm the diagnosis retrospectively; cross-reactive across Orthopoxvirus genus.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw is taken acutely and ideally again 2–3 weeks later; paired samples showing seroconversion support the diagnosis.

diagnosticTests.test: HIV test and immune function screen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies occult immunodeficiency that may explain unusually severe disease and guides treatment decisions regarding antiviral therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw for HIV fourth-generation antigen/antibody test, full blood count, and lymphocyte subsets; results typically available within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Isolates live CPXV for definitive characterisation, including whole-genome sequencing to classify the CPXV strain and investigate outbreak linkage.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: PCR-positive samples are forwarded to a reference virology laboratory; culture takes several days and requires appropriate biological containment.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin biopsy with histopathology and immunohistochemistry
diagnosticTests.purpose: In unusual presentations, skin biopsy can show characteristic orthopoxvirus intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies (Guarnieri bodies) and confirm poxvirus aetiology when other tests are inconclusive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small punch or shave biopsy is performed under local anaesthetic; fixed tissue is stained and examined microscopically, with results in 3–5 days.

treatmentOverview: In immunocompetent individuals with localised cutaneous cowpox, the infection is self-limiting and treatment is supportive. The lesion progresses through its stages and heals spontaneously over 3–10 weeks regardless of antiviral therapy, and no randomised trial evidence supports routine antiviral use in this group. Supportive management includes wound care (keeping the lesion clean, covered with non-adherent dressings, and protected from trauma or secondary infection), oral analgesics for pain, and treatment of regional lymphadenopathy symptoms. Systemic symptoms such as fever and malaise are managed with paracetamol or ibuprofen. Patients should be advised against rubbing or touching their eyes after touching the lesion, to prevent autoinoculation of the cornea. The lesion should be covered in contacts with young children, immunocompromised people, or those with eczema to prevent onward transmission. In immunocompromised patients, patients with atopic eczema and extensive cutaneous disease, those with eye involvement, or those with evidence of dissemination, antiviral therapy is indicated. Tecovirimat (TPOXX) is the first-choice antiviral based on its established activity against orthopoxviruses, tolerability, and oral bioavailability; it is used off-label for cowpox in the same dosing schedule as for mpox treatment (weight-based, twice daily for 14 days). Vaccinia immune globulin intravenous (VIGIV) and cidofovir or brincidofovir are second-line options for severe cases where tecovirimat is unavailable or has failed. Ophthalmological review is essential if ocular involvement is present. Vaccination with MVA-BN (Jynneos) is appropriate post-exposure prophylaxis for unvaccinated close contacts in household or occupational exposure settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Wound care and supportive management
medicalTreatments.description: The mainstay of treatment for localised cowpox in immunocompetent patients: keeping the lesion clean with antiseptic solution, covering with non-adherent dressings, preventing scratching or manipulation that could cause secondary infection or autoinoculation, and maintaining good hand hygiene throughout the illness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appropriate wound care reduces secondary bacterial superinfection risk by approximately 40–60% and prevents the majority of autoinoculation complications; the lesion will heal spontaneously in virtually 100% of immunocompetent patients without antivirals.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral analgesics (paracetamol/ibuprofen)
medicalTreatments.description: Cowpox lesions are often disproportionately painful; regular oral analgesics (paracetamol 1 g four times daily, or ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily with food) manage local and systemic pain effectively during the pustular and haemorrhagic eschar stages.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate analgesia achieves satisfactory pain control in approximately 80–90% of patients in the acute phase without requiring additional intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Tecovirimat (TPOXX) for severe or at-risk cases
medicalTreatments.description: An Orthopoxvirus antiviral that inhibits VP37 viral egress protein; used off-label for immunocompromised patients, those with atopic eczema and extensive disease, ocular involvement, or suspected dissemination. Administered orally, weight-based dosing twice daily for 14 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational data from cowpox and mpox show lesion resolution improvement in approximately 60–80% of immunocompromised patients treated with tecovirimat versus the natural history; no randomised controlled trial for cowpox specifically.

medicalTreatments.name: Antibiotics for secondary bacterial superinfection
medicalTreatments.description: Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes can superinfect the deep ulcerating cowpox eschar; topical mupirocin for mild superficial infection or systemic antibiotics (flucloxacillin, co-amoxiclav, or doxycycline depending on culture and sensitivity) for more extensive secondary infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Appropriate antibiotic therapy resolves secondary bacterial skin infection in approximately 85–95% of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: MVA-BN (Jynneos) post-exposure vaccination
medicalTreatments.description: Post-exposure vaccination of unvaccinated close household or occupational contacts within 4 days of significant exposure may prevent or reduce severity of cowpox infection; recommended for immunocompromised household contacts who cannot receive the live ACAM2000 vaccine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Based on cross-protection data from smallpox and mpox, MVA-BN post-exposure prophylaxis is estimated to reduce risk of symptomatic orthopoxvirus infection by approximately 78–85%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical antiseptic dressings
alternativeTreatments.description: Silver-containing or povidone-iodine dressings applied to the open lesion reduce microbial colonisation and can speed the secondary healing phase after the eschar separates; used as an adjunct to standard wound care.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ophthalmological assessment and topical antivirals if ocular cowpox
alternativeTreatments.description: If lesions involve the eyelid or conjunctiva, urgent ophthalmological review is needed; topical trifluridine or cidofovir eye drops have been used in cases of orthopoxvirus ocular infection to prevent corneal ulceration and vision loss.

whySeeSpecialist: Most localised cowpox in healthy adults can be managed by a GP with appropriate wound care and analgesics after laboratory confirmation. An infectious disease specialist or dermatologist should be involved if the diagnosis is uncertain, if the patient is immunocompromised or has atopic eczema with more than one lesion, if there is any systemic spread beyond a single lesion, or if the eye is involved. Public health notification is not mandatory for cowpox in most jurisdictions but reporting to local health protection teams helps with surveillance and source investigation.
doctorSelectionGuide: For routine localised cowpox, a GP with access to reference virology testing for PCR confirmation is sufficient. For immunocompromised patients or suspected disseminated disease, an infectious disease physician familiar with poxvirus infections is preferable; specialist centres with experience in exotic viral infections are found in university hospital settings. For ocular involvement, an ophthalmologist experienced in infectious keratitis should assess the patient within 24 hours.
preventionStrategies: Wear gloves when handling cats with skin lesions, particularly on the paws or muzzle, until the cause has been identified.
Seek veterinary assessment promptly for cats with unusual or ulcerating skin lesions to allow both animal treatment and owner protection.
Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water after any contact with cats, pet rodents, or exotic animals.

Consider pre-exposure vaccination with MVA-BN (Jynneos) for high-risk occupational groups including zoo animal handlers, veterinarians with exotic animal practice, and laboratory workers handling CPXV.
Inform your doctor about animal contact when presenting with any unusual skin lesion, particularly a dark haemorrhagic eschar on the hands or face.
Rodent control in rural properties reduces the risk of infected wild rodents entering gardens or outbuildings where cats can become exposed.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid rubbing your eyes or touching mucous membranes while you have an active cowpox lesion; autoinoculation of the cornea can cause sight-threatening keratitis.
Cover the lesion with a dressing and avoid skin-to-skin contact with household members who have eczema or are immunocompromised until the lesion has fully healed.
Do not pop, squeeze, or manipulate the lesion; this delays healing, increases scarring, and risks spreading the virus to other skin areas.
Wash bedding and clothing that has contacted the lesion at 60°C or higher.
Keep the infected cat or animal isolated from other pets and people until veterinary assessment confirms the lesion is healing or an alternative diagnosis is made.

recommended: Adequate protein intake to support skin healing during the weeks-long lesion cycle
Vitamin C-rich foods (citrus, berries, peppers) which support wound healing and immune function
Sufficient hydration to support fever management in the systemic phase
Zinc-containing foods (meat, seeds, legumes) which are important for skin barrier repair
avoid: Alcohol during the febrile phase, which increases dehydration and impairs immune function
Unnecessary immunosuppressants or high-dose corticosteroids that could worsen disease severity

exerciseGuidelines: Light activity is appropriate during localised cowpox once systemic symptoms (fever, significant malaise) have settled, typically within 1–2 weeks. The lesion itself does not limit aerobic exercise, but activities that risk trauma to the lesion, contact sports involving skin-to-skin contact, or swimming in public pools should be avoided until all crusts have separated and new skin has formed, to prevent both injury and onward transmission.
dailyManagement: Change the wound dressing daily (or more frequently if it becomes saturated), cleansing the lesion with saline or dilute antiseptic before reapplying a fresh non-adherent dressing.

Inspect the lesion for signs of secondary bacterial infection: increasing redness, warmth, pain, or purulent exudate that differs from the expected haemorrhagic crust; seek reassessment if these develop.
Track lesion size by measuring its diameter weekly; a lesion that is expanding significantly beyond 3–4 cm or showing new satellite lesions warrants reassessment.
Maintain a record of any systemic symptoms (new fever, cough, eye symptoms) and report these promptly to your GP or specialist.
Keep the affected cat or source animal under veterinary supervision and inform any other people who may have had contact with the animal.
prognosis: In immunocompetent individuals, cowpox has an excellent prognosis. The localised lesion heals completely in 3–10 weeks in virtually all cases without antiviral treatment. A permanent scar at the inoculation site is the most common long-term sequela, occurring in the majority of cases due to the depth of the haemorrhagic eschar. Systemic illness (fever, malaise, lymphadenopathy) resolves within 1–3 weeks. Second infections are unusual because the immune response to orthopoxviruses is robust. In immunocompromised patients, the prognosis is considerably more uncertain; several fatalities from disseminated cowpox and cowpox pneumonia have been reported in Europe, predominantly in patients with haematological malignancies, HIV, organ transplant recipients, and those with combined immunodeficiency. Severe or disseminated disease in this group warrants prompt antiviral treatment to reduce the risk of a fatal outcome. People with atopic eczema can experience more widespread cutaneous disease but rarely develop visceral spread.
recoveryTimeline: The full natural history of a single cowpox lesion in an immunocompetent adult spans approximately 3–10 weeks from inoculation to complete healing. The incubation period after animal contact is approximately 9–10 days (range 3–21 days). The initial papule appears at days 9–10, vesicles form over days 10–14, pustules and haemorrhagic crusting occur over days 14–25, the eschar is present and gradually detaches over days 20–45, and complete re-epithelialisation with residual scarring occurs by weeks 6–10. Systemic symptoms typically resolve by weeks 2–3. Immunocompromised patients may have a prolonged and more complicated course lasting months without antiviral treatment.
complications: Permanent scar at the inoculation site — occurs in the majority of cases due to the depth and haemorrhagic nature of the cowpox eschar.
Secondary bacterial skin infection (cellulitis, abscess) — from Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes, more likely when the lesion is manipulated or inadequately dressed.
Ocular cowpox — vesicular or ulcerating lesions on the eyelid or cornea from autoinoculation; can cause keratitis and, in severe cases, permanent corneal scarring or vision loss.
Disseminated cowpox in immunocompromised patients — multiple cutaneous lesions beyond the inoculation site, visceral involvement, and potentially fatal outcome, particularly with lung involvement.
Eczema cowpox — widespread vesicular eruption in patients with atopic dermatitis, potentially severe and requiring antiviral treatment.
Psychological impact of visible scarring — particularly when lesions involve the face; may require dermatological or psychological follow-up.

supportResources.name: UK Health Security Agency — Cowpox Guidance for Clinicians
supportResources.description: Clinical guidance from the UK Health Security Agency on diagnosis, management, and public health response to human cowpox cases.

supportResources.name: CDC — Poxvirus Information (Cowpox)
supportResources.description: U.S. Centers for Disease Control overview of poxvirus infections including cowpox, providing information for healthcare providers and the public.

supportResources.name: ECDC — Cowpox technical guidance
supportResources.description: European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control information on cowpox epidemiology, risk factors, and surveillance in Europe.

sources.title: Ninove L et al. Cowpox virus infection in humans: a review in light of increasing evidence. Clinical Microbiology and Infection, 2009
Vorou RM, Papavassiliou VG, Pierroutsakos IN. Cowpox virus infection: an emerging health threat. Current Opinion in Infectious Diseases, 2008
Baxby D, Bennett M, Getty B. Human cowpox 1969–93: a review based on 54 cases. British Journal of Dermatology, 1994
Campe H et al. Cowpox virus transmission from pet rats to humans, Germany, 2009. Emerging Infectious Diseases, 2009

Essbauer S et al. Zoonotic poxviruses. Veterinary Microbiology, 2010
Grosenbach DW et al. Oral tecovirimat for the treatment of smallpox. New England Journal of Medicine, 2018 (basis for cowpox antiviral use)

confusedWithConditions.name: Anthrax (cutaneous)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cutaneous anthrax produces a painless black eschar surrounded by oedema without vesicular progression or systemic fever at the outset; cowpox lesions are very painful, vesicular before becoming haemorrhagic, and associated with lymphadenopathy and fever. Anthrax arises from contact with animal hides or spores, not cats.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Herpes simplex produces clusters of small vesicles on an erythematous base at mucocutaneous sites; it does not progress to a single deep haemorrhagic black eschar, and is not acquired from animal contact.

confusedWithConditions.name: Monkeypox (mpox)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mpox causes multiple synchronous lesions throughout the body with prominent systemic illness; cowpox typically causes a single or a few localised lesions at the inoculation site. PCR is required to distinguish CPXV from MPXV definitively.

relatedConditions.name: Monkeypox (mpox)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are Orthopoxvirus infections producing similar vesiculopustular rashes; cowpox is localised and acquired from cats/rodents, mpox is typically more systemic. Vaccination with MVA-BN protects against both.

relatedConditions.name: Smallpox
relatedConditions.relevance: Cowpox is historically significant as the basis of Jenner's smallpox vaccine; cross-reactive orthopoxvirus immunity from prior smallpox vaccination confers protection against cowpox.

relatedConditions.name: Anthrax
relatedConditions.relevance: Cutaneous anthrax is the primary differential diagnosis for the black eschar of cowpox; both present with a dark ulcerating lesion after animal contact but are caused by entirely different pathogens.

faqs.question: What is cowpox?
faqs.answer: Cowpox is a rare viral skin infection caused by Cowpox virus, an Orthopoxvirus carried naturally by wild rodents in Europe. It is most often caught from domestic cats that hunt rodents. It causes a single painful dark lesion at the site of animal contact that heals in 3–10 weeks. Despite the name, cows are not the main source.

faqs.question: How do you catch cowpox?
faqs.answer: Most people catch cowpox from a bite, scratch, or lick from an infected domestic cat. The cat itself catches the virus from wild rodents. Less commonly, cowpox is caught from pet rodents (rats, hamsters), zoo animals such as elephants and big cats, or — rarely — from infected cattle.

faqs.question: What does a cowpox lesion look like?
faqs.answer: A cowpox lesion starts as a red spot that becomes a fluid-filled blister, then a pus-filled pustule, and finally a distinctive dark red to black haemorrhagic crust (eschar) on a hard, inflamed base up to 3–4 cm wide. This striking black eschar is the hallmark of cowpox and is usually found on the hands, arms, or face.

faqs.question: Is cowpox dangerous?
faqs.answer: For most healthy adults, cowpox is a self-limiting infection that heals completely in 3–10 weeks without specific treatment. It is significantly more dangerous for immunocompromised people — including those with HIV, on chemotherapy, or taking immunosuppressants — in whom it can spread widely and has caused deaths. People with eczema are also at greater risk of severe disease.

faqs.question: Is there a treatment for cowpox?
faqs.answer: There is no specific antiviral treatment needed for healthy adults with localised cowpox. The lesion heals on its own with wound care and pain relief. For immunocompromised patients or severe disease, tecovirimat (TPOXX) is used off-label as an antiviral. Post-exposure vaccination with the MVA-BN (Jynneos) vaccine may prevent or reduce disease in exposed contacts.

faqs.question: How long does cowpox last?
faqs.answer: A cowpox lesion in a healthy adult takes 3–10 weeks to heal completely from the time it appears. The dark black eschar typically develops 2–3 weeks after the initial bite or scratch, persists for 2–4 more weeks, then separates, leaving a permanent scar. Systemic symptoms like fever and swollen glands usually resolve within 1–3 weeks.

faqs.question: Can you catch cowpox from a cat?
faqs.answer: Yes. Domestic cats are currently the most common source of human cowpox in Europe. Cats pick up Cowpox virus from hunting wild rodents and can then transmit it to people through bites, scratches, or contact with active lesions on their paws or face. Cats with unusual sores on their paws or muzzle should be assessed by a vet.

faqs.question: Does the smallpox vaccine protect against cowpox?
faqs.answer: Yes. Smallpox vaccines based on vaccinia virus (such as MVA-BN/Jynneos or ACAM2000) provide substantial cross-protection against cowpox because all three viruses are closely related Orthopoxviruses with shared antigens. People who received smallpox vaccination before 1980 retain partial protection. MVA-BN vaccination is recommended for high-risk occupational groups.

faqs.question: How is cowpox diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Cowpox is diagnosed by PCR testing of a swab taken from the lesion, which detects Cowpox virus DNA and distinguishes it from other poxviruses. Electron microscopy of blister fluid can confirm an Orthopoxvirus infection quickly. Blood tests for orthopoxvirus antibodies and immune function tests to check for hidden immunosuppression are also performed.

faqs.question: Is cowpox contagious from person to person?
faqs.answer: Human-to-human transmission of cowpox has been reported but is uncommon. The main risk is from direct contact with an active lesion or its secretions — covering the lesion and good hand hygiene substantially reduces this risk. People in your household who are immunocompromised or have eczema are most at risk and should avoid contact with the lesion.

faqs.question: Can I still have my cat if it has cowpox?
faqs.answer: You do not need to euthanise a cat with cowpox. The cat will usually recover on its own. However, wear gloves when handling it, wash hands after any contact, and keep it away from anyone who is immunocompromised or has eczema until its lesions have healed. Get veterinary advice promptly to confirm the diagnosis and treat any secondary infection.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cowpox and monkeypox?
faqs.answer: Cowpox causes a single localised haemorrhagic pustule at the site of animal contact, usually acquired from a cat or rodent, and does not spread throughout the body in healthy people. Monkeypox (mpox) causes multiple synchronous lesions across the body with more prominent systemic illness and is now spread mainly through sexual contact in many countries.

faqs.question: Does cowpox cause permanent scars?
faqs.answer: Yes, in most cases. The deep haemorrhagic eschar of cowpox heals by scarring, leaving a permanent pitted or atrophic mark at the inoculation site, similar to a smallpox vaccination scar. The scar is usually round and located on the hand, arm, or face where the animal contact occurred.

faqs.question: What should I do if a cat bites me and I develop a dark sore?
faqs.answer: See your GP promptly and mention the cat bite or contact. A dark, painful, enlarging sore appearing 1–3 weeks after an animal bite should be swabbed for PCR testing to check for cowpox and other poxvirus infections. Keep the lesion covered and clean while awaiting results. If you are immunocompromised or have eczema, seek same-day advice.

faqs.question: How did cowpox lead to the first vaccine?
faqs.answer: Edward Jenner observed in 1796 that milkmaids who caught cowpox from infected cows were protected against smallpox. He demonstrated that inoculating people with cowpox material prevented smallpox infection. This was the world's first vaccine — the word vaccine comes from 'vacca', the Latin for cow — and paved the way for the eventual global eradication of smallpox in 1980.

faqs.question: Can children get cowpox?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children can catch cowpox through contact with infected cats or pet rodents and are at particular risk because they play closely with animals and may not wash their hands carefully. Children with atopic eczema are at higher risk of more severe disease and should have any unusual skin lesion after animal contact assessed by a doctor the same day.

faqs.question: Is cowpox present in the United States?
faqs.answer: No naturally occurring Cowpox virus circulates in North America. Cowpox is endemic in Europe and parts of Asia but has never been established in the USA or Australasia. Any poxvirus skin infection in North America after animal contact is more likely to be from a related Orthopoxvirus or parapoxvirus, and PCR testing should specify which virus is present.

faqs.question: When should I go to hospital for cowpox?
faqs.answer: Go to hospital urgently if you have any new skin lesions appearing away from the original bite site, any respiratory symptoms such as cough or shortness of breath, any change in vision or eye pain, or if you are immunocompromised and your single lesion is not healing normally within 3 weeks. Contact your GP or infectious disease team before attending a general emergency department.

faqs.question: Can cowpox spread to other parts of my body?
faqs.answer: In healthy people, cowpox almost always stays as a single localised lesion and does not spread. Self-spread can occur through touching the lesion then rubbing your eyes (risking corneal infection) or touching other skin areas. In immunocompromised patients, the virus can spread internally, causing multiple lesions and lung disease — this is why identifying and treating immunodeficiency is important.

faqs.question: What animals carry cowpox besides cats?
faqs.answer: Wild rodents — particularly bank voles and wood mice in Europe — are the natural reservoir of Cowpox virus. Domestic cats, pet rats and hamsters, zoo elephants, rhinoceroses, big cats, and other exotic mammals can all be infected and transmit the virus to humans. Cows and cattle, despite the historical name, are only occasional accidental hosts.

faqs.question: Is cowpox related to mpox and smallpox?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cowpox, mpox (monkeypox), and smallpox are all caused by different members of the Orthopoxvirus genus. They share common antigens, which is why smallpox and mpox vaccines also protect against cowpox. Cowpox is the mildest of the three in healthy people, smallpox was the most lethal (now eradicated), and mpox sits between them in severity.

metaDescription: Cowpox causes a painful dark pustular scar usually from an infected cat bite. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, who is at risk, and when treatment is needed.
